answer.rationale: Matt 6:19-21 forbids laying up treasures on earth and locates the believer's treasure in heaven; Matt 19:23-24 declares it hard for the rich to enter the kingdom; Luke 6:20-26 pronounces blessing on the literally poor and woe on the literally rich; Phil 4:11-13 frames Paul's autarkeia (G842) as learned contentment in both lack and abundance, scoping the famous panta of 4:13 to circumstantial endurance rather than acquisition; 1 Tim 6:5-10 names those who think eusebeia (godliness) is a means of porismos (gain) as deceived, and identifies philargyria (G5365, love of money) as a root of all kinds of evils that has caused some to wander apo tes pisteos (from the faith); 2 Pet 2:3 exposes false teachers who exploit followers with feigned words for gain (pleonexia). The pan-canonical lemma pattern (thesauros, ptochos, plousios, philargyria) is unambiguous: the gospel of Christ crucified does not promise material wealth or physical health as a covenantal fruit of saving faith.
answer.notes: Scoping is decisive. The verdict targets the outright prosperity teaching that material wealth and physical health are covenantal promises of the gospel, obtainable by faith-confession, sowing seed-faith offerings, or positive confession. Many orthodox Christians who happen to enjoy material blessing, who affirm biblical stewardship, or who pray for healing in faith are NOT prosperity-gospel adherents and are not in view. Public-record carriers of the prosperity teaching whose denial places them outside this verdict: Kenneth Hagin Sr. (Word of Faith founder), Kenneth Copeland, Joel Osteen, Creflo Dollar, Benny Hinn, Pat Robertson, Paula White, Jesse Duplantis, the late E. W. Kenyon (theological forerunner), and the broader Word-of-Faith / health-and-wealth movement strongly present in West Africa, Brazil, Korea, and the Philippines. T. D. Jakes and Joyce Meyer are mixed cases with some prosperity teaching present. Importantly, classical Pentecostal bodies including the Assemblies of God formally distance themselves from extreme prosperity teaching while affirming divine healing and provision; the AG is NOT in view here. Hank Hanegraaff's Christianity in Crisis (1993, updated 21st-century edition) is the most cited orthodox refutation; the Lausanne Movement's 2009 Akropong Theology Working Group consultation and the 2010 Cape Town Commitment publicly named prosperity teaching as false. Because the prosperity message redefines the gospel itself (adding material-prosperity-promise to the cross-centered gospel of grace) and assigns to faith a magical-causation function that makes God a servant of human acquisition, Galatians 1:8-9's apostolic anathema against another gospel applies. The cross of Christ (the suffering Saviour) is the foundational refutation: a crucified Lord whose apostles were impoverished, beaten, hungry, and martyred (2 Cor 11:23-29, Heb 11:35-38) cannot be the patron of a wealth-and-health gospel.

stem_audit.notes: The question statement is descriptive: it defines the gospel positively as forgiveness of sins through Christ's death and resurrection and denies that God guarantees material wealth or physical health as a fruit of saving faith. It names no heretics and uses no Reformed-confessional jargon. The statement is testable against the lexical pattern.

lay_summary.reasoning: Yes. The gospel is the good news that sins are forgiven because Jesus died and rose, and God does not promise that faithful Christians will be wealthy or always physically healthy in this life. The strongest reason from Scripture is the consistent pattern across the New Testament. Jesus says in Matthew 6:19-21 do not store up treasures on earth, store them in heaven. In Matthew 19:23-24 he says it is hard for a rich man to enter the kingdom of heaven. In Luke 6:20-26 he blesses the poor and pronounces woe on the rich and the full and the laughing. Paul in 1 Timothy 6:5-10 names people who treat godliness as a way to get rich, and he says the love of money is a root of all kinds of evils and some who craved it wandered away from the faith. Paul himself in 2 Corinthians 11 lists beatings, imprisonments, hunger, and shipwreck as the evidence of his apostleship, not wealth and comfort. Hebrews 11 ends its hall-of-faith chapter with heroes who were tortured, stoned, sawn in two, destitute, and afflicted. The strongest counterargument prosperity teachers cite is Philippians 4:13: I can do all things through him who strengthens me. They read this as a promise of unlimited success. But the immediate context, verses 11-12, is Paul saying he has learned to be content whether brought low or abounding, whether hungry or full. The all things he can do through Christ is endurance in both poverty and plenty, not acquisition of riches. Teachers also point to Old Testament covenant blessings of land and prosperity in Deuteronomy 28, but those were tied to the Sinai covenant with national Israel, not to the gospel of Christ. The verdict handles the tension cleanly. God is generous, prayer for healing is right and biblical, and material provision is given as God wills. But material wealth and physical health are nowhere guaranteed by saving faith. The cross itself, a suffering Saviour, is the deepest refutation of any health-and-wealth promise.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: On this proposition the historic Christian lineages are in overwhelming agreement. Patristic teaching from Clement of Alexandria, Tertullian, Cyprian, Augustine, and the desert ascetic tradition emphasized voluntary poverty, almsgiving, and detachment from wealth as marks of discipleship, never as the fruit of saving faith. Catholic teaching in the Catechism and in papal social encyclicals warns explicitly against confusing material possession with human or spiritual fulfillment; the beatitudes of poverty of spirit and the dominical saying that one cannot serve both God and mammon stand at the centre of moral theology. Lutheran theology centres on the theology of the cross and the gospel as forgiveness of sins through Christ alone, with worldly suffering as the normal Christian lot rather than worldly prosperity. Anglican Article XI and Methodist Article IX of religion state that believers are accounted righteous before God for the merit of Christ alone, by faith, not for any earthly reward. Reformed and Anabaptist traditions have been historically severe against any equation of riches with godliness. Eastern Orthodoxy preserves the ancient ascetic and monastic witness, treating voluntary poverty, fasting, and almsgiving as central practices of the Christian life. Classical Pentecostal bodies including the Assemblies of God formally distance themselves from extreme prosperity teaching while affirming divine healing and provision, and the global Lausanne Movement at Cape Town 2010 publicly named prosperity gospel as false teaching. The denial line runs through the modern Word of Faith movement, including Kenneth Hagin, Kenneth Copeland, Joel Osteen, Creflo Dollar, Benny Hinn, Paula White, Jesse Duplantis, and the broader prosperity-gospel networks strongly present in West Africa, Brazil, Korea, and the Philippines. Hank Hanegraaff's Christianity in Crisis is the most widely cited orthodox refutation. The historic Christian witness on this question is one of the most united across all lineages.

scripture.key_term: θησαυρός (thesauros) G2344; θησαυρίζω (thesaurizo) G2343; γῆ (ge) G1093; οὐρανός (ouranos) G3772; καρδία (kardia) G2588
scripture.force: Imperative prohibition: me thesaurizete… epi tes ges ('do not lay up treasures on the earth'); contrast clause thesaurizete… en ourano ('lay up treasures in heaven'); concluding gnomic clause locates the heart where the treasure is; the lexical pattern positions earthly treasure-accumulation as antithetical to kingdom orientation

scripture.key_term: πλούσιος (plousios) G4145; δυσκόλως (dyskolos) G1423; εἰσέρχομαι (eiserchomai) G1525; βασιλεία (basileia) G932; κάμηλος (kamelos) G2574; τρύπημα ῥαφίδος (trupema rhaphidos) G5169/G4476
scripture.force: Comparative hyperbole: kamelos through trupema rhaphidos easier than plousios entering basileia tou theou; the saying does not denounce wealth as such but its near-impossible compatibility with kingdom entry apart from the act of God in v.26; complicates any teaching that posits wealth as a sign of God's favour under the gospel

scripture.key_term: μακάριος (makarios) G3107; πτωχός (ptochos) G4434; οὐαί (ouai) G3759; πλούσιος (plousios) G4145; παράκλησις (paraklesis) G3874
scripture.force: Lukan beatitudes use literal ptochoi (the destitute) with no spiritual qualifier (contrast Matt 5:3 ptochoi to pneumati); paired ouai on the literally plousioi who 'have received their paraklesis (consolation) already'; the chiastic blessing-woe structure inverts prosperity-gospel expectation at the lexical level

scripture.key_term: αὐτάρκης (autarkes) G842; ταπεινοῦσθαι (tapeinousthai) G5013; περισσεύειν (perisseuein) G4052; πάντα (panta) G3956; ἐνδυναμοῦντί (endynamounti) G1743
scripture.force: Paul declares he has learned autarkes (self-sufficient contentment) in whatever state; the paired infinitives tapeinousthai ('to be brought low') and perisseuein ('to abound') frame the panta of v.13; therefore panta ischyo en to endynamounti me ('I can do all things through him who strengthens me') is scoped by the immediate context to endurance in poverty AND plenty, not to acquisition of prosperity; the famous prosperity-gospel proof-text exegetically reverses its own usage

scripture.key_term: πορισμός (porismos) G4200; εὐσέβεια (eusebeia) G2150; φιλαργυρία (philargyria) G5365; ῥίζα (rhiza) G4491; βουλόμενοι πλουτεῖν (boulomenoi ploutein) G1014/G4147; ἀπεπλανήθησαν ἀπὸ τῆς πίστεως (apeplanethesan apo tes pisteos) G635/G4102; ὀδύναι (odynai) G3601
scripture.force: Direct apostolic naming of the prosperity error: nomizonton porismon einai ten eusebeian ('thinking godliness is a means of gain'); the rhiza panton ton kakon ('root of all kinds of evils') is philargyria, the love of money; hes tines oregomenoi apeplanethesan apo tes pisteos ('craving which some have wandered from the faith'); the lexical pattern places those who treat faith as a means to wealth outside the faith they claim

scripture.key_term: φιλάργυρος (philargyros) G5366; ἔχοντες μόρφωσιν εὐσεβείας τὴν δὲ δύναμιν αὐτῆς ἠρνημένοι (echontes morphosin eusebeias ten de dynamin autes ernemenoi) G3446/G2150/G1411/G720
scripture.force: Last-days characterization of false teachers includes philargyros ('lovers of money') alongside boasters, proud, blasphemers; they have a morphosis (outward form) of eusebeia but have denied (ernemenoi, perfect middle participle of arneomai) its dynamis (power); the form-without-power pattern matches the prosperity-stage spectacle without gospel substance

scripture.key_term: ψευδοδιδάσκαλοι (pseudodidaskaloi) G5572; πλαστοῖς λόγοις (plastois logois) G4112/G3056; ἐμπορεύσονται (emporeusontai) G1710; πλεονεξία (pleonexia) G4124
scripture.force: False teachers (pseudodidaskaloi) will exploit (emporeusontai, future middle of emporeuomai, 'to traffic in, make merchandise of') believers with plastoi logoi ('feigned, fabricated words'); the motive is pleonexia ('covetousness, greed'); the apostolic warning predicts the very commercialization of the gospel that prosperity teaching exemplifies

scripture.key_term: ταπεινός (tapeinos) G5011; ὕψωσις (hypsosis) G5311; πλούσιος (plousios) G4145; ταπείνωσις (tapeinosis) G5014
scripture.force: Chiastic reversal: the tapeinos brother boasts in his hypsosis (exaltation), the plousios in his tapeinosis (humiliation, because he will pass like the grass that withers); the lexical pattern inverts the world's value scheme and forecloses any gospel teaching that treats wealth as the visible mark of divine favour

scripture.key_term: αἰτέω (aiteo) G154; κακῶς (kakos) G2560; δαπανήσητε (dapanesete) G1159; ἡδοναῖς (hedonais) G2237
scripture.force: Petition formula directly addressing the prosperity-prayer assumption: aiteite kai ou lambanete dioti kakos aiteisthe ('you ask and do not receive because you ask wrongly') hina en tais hedonais hymon dapanesete ('that you may spend it on your pleasures'); the corruption of the petition by self-indulgent motive is what disqualifies it; the lexical pattern refutes name-it-and-claim-it as a misuse of prayer

scripture.key_term: κόποι (kopoi) G2873; φυλακαί (phylakai) G5438; πληγαί (plegai) G4127; λιμός καὶ δίψος (limos kai dipsos) G3042/G1373; ἀσθένεια (astheneia) G769
scripture.force: Paul's apostolic credentials are a catalog of suffering: kopoi (toils), phylakai (imprisonments), plegai (beatings), limos kai dipsos (hunger and thirst), astheneia (weakness); the inverted CV refutes any gospel reading that treats material wealth and physical health as marks of authentic apostleship

scripture.key_term: ἐτυμπανίσθησαν (etympanisthesan) G5178; ἐλιθάσθησαν (elithasthesan) G3034; ἐπρίσθησαν (epristhesan) G4249; ὑστερούμενοι θλιβόμενοι κακουχούμενοι (hysteroumenoi thlibomenoi kakouchoumenoi) G5302/G2346/G2558
scripture.force: The hall-of-faith concludes with believers tortured, mocked, scourged, stoned, sawn in two, killed by the sword, destitute, afflicted, mistreated, wandering in deserts; the cumulative lexical pattern frames faith's hallmark as endurance through deprivation, not arrival into prosperity

scripture.key_term: πλούσιος (plousios) G4145; πεπλούτηκα (peploutekā) G4147; ταλαίπωρος καὶ ἐλεεινός καὶ πτωχός καὶ τυφλός καὶ γυμνός (talaiporos kai eleeinos kai ptochos kai typhlos kai gymnos) G5005/G1652/G4434/G5185/G1131
scripture.force: Laodicea's self-assessment plousios eimi kai peploutekā ('I am rich and have become wealthy') is exposed by the risen Christ as ptochos kai typhlos kai gymnos ('poor and blind and naked'); the lexical inversion in apocalyptic-symbolism shows that material wealth can co-exist with spiritual destitution, a direct rebuke of equating prosperity with God's favour

Mosaic covenant blessings list material prosperity (fruit of the womb, ground, livestock, basket and kneading bowl) as conditional fruit of covenant obedience for national Israel under the Sinai administration; complicates the verdict only if read as still operative under the new covenant; the new-covenant lexical pattern (Matt 6:19-21, 1 Tim 6:5-10) reorients reward to the age to come and refuses to project Sinai material-blessing language onto gospel-era faith

John's epistolary greeting peri panton euchomai se euodousthai kai hygiainein ('I pray that in all respects you may prosper and be in good health'); a standard ancient-letter health-wish formula, not a covenantal promise; complicates the verdict only when extracted from genre and weaponized as a universal gospel pledge; the lexical force is wish-prayer not guarantee

scripture.key_term: ἑκατονταπλασίονα (hekatontaplasiona) G1542; μετὰ διωγμῶν (meta diogmon) G3326/G1375
scripture.force: Hundredfold return to the disciple is qualified by meta diogmon ('with persecutions') and eis ton aiona ton erchomenon zoen aionion ('in the age to come, eternal life'); complicates a prosperity reading by including persecution in the very promise, and locating the eternal portion in the age to come, not now

scripture.key_term: ἀνάθεμα (anathema) G331; εὐαγγέλιον (euangelion) G2098; παρ' ὃ (par' ho) G3844
scripture.force: Apostolic anathema falls on any preaching of a euangelion par' ho ('beyond what') the apostles preached; the lexical force is decisive against any teaching that redefines the gospel by adding a material-prosperity-promise to it

competing_lens_verdicts.note: The Mosaic material blessings of Deut 28 are typological and find their fulfillment in Christ and in the new-creation inheritance; under the new covenant the believer's portion is Christ himself, and outward prosperity is not a covenantal sign

Deut 28 prosperity is administratively tied to the Sinai covenant with national Israel; the church-age dispensation operates under grace not Mosaic terms; future millennial blessings remain in view for ethnic Israel under that interpretation but do not retroject onto present-age church faith as a prosperity promise

Same verdict as covenantal; Christ's sermon on the mount and the apostolic epistles redefine treasure, blessing, and reward eschatologically, and the new covenant carries no material-prosperity pledge

Reads Deut 28, 3 John 2, Mark 10:30, and Phil 4:13 as universal covenantal promises; treats faith as a metaphysical force that activates material results; the lens collapses progressive revelation and ignores the immediate contextual scoping of panta in Phil 4:13 and the qualifier 'with persecutions' in Mark 10:30; this lens is the one under test

hermeneutics.notes: The verdict converges across the orthodox lenses (covenantal, dispensational, new-covenant theology, progressive covenantalism) because the New Testament lexical pattern is uniform across thesauros, ptochos, plousios, philargyria, and pleonexia. The prosperity hermeneutic is the only lens producing a contrary verdict, and it does so by detaching anchor texts from their immediate context (especially Phil 4:13) and by treating Mosaic material-blessing language as pan-canonically operative. Analogia scripturae is decisive: the apostolic CV in 2 Cor 11, the heroes of Heb 11, the warning of 1 Tim 6, and the dominical sayings in Matt 6 and Luke 6 together foreclose the prosperity reading.

counter_witness.anchor: Clement of Alexandria, Quis Dives Salvetur? (Who is the Rich Man That Shall Be Saved?), c. 200 AD
counter_witness.key_phrase: Riches are not in themselves to be cast away, but the passions of the soul which hinder the right use of them; salvation is not by possession or non-possession but by right disposition of heart toward Christ.

counter_witness.anchor: Augustine, Sermon 85 on Matt 19:23 and City of God 1.10
counter_witness.key_phrase: The good things of this life are not the rewards of true godliness, for they are given alike to the good and the evil; only the eternal inheritance distinguishes the children of God.

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church, paragraphs 2544-2547 on poverty of heart
counter_witness.key_phrase: Jesus enjoins his disciples to prefer him to everything and everyone, and bids them to renounce all that they have for his sake and that of the Gospel; abandonment to the providence of the Father frees us from anxiety about tomorrow.

counter_witness.anchor: John Paul II, Sollicitudo Rei Socialis, 1987
counter_witness.key_phrase: The more one possesses the more one wants, while deeper aspirations remain unsatisfied and perhaps even stifled. Authentic development cannot be reduced to the multiplication of goods.

Men are freely justified for Christ's sake, through faith, when they believe that they are received into favor, and that their sins are forgiven for Christ's sake. The gospel is forgiveness, not material reward.

counter_witness.anchor: Luther, Heidelberg Disputation 1518, theses 19-21, theology of the cross
counter_witness.key_phrase: He is not rightly called a theologian who looks upon the invisible things of God as though they were clearly perceptible in things which have actually happened. The theologian of glory calls evil good and good evil. The theologian of the cross calls the thing what it actually is.

counter_witness.anchor: 39 Articles, Article XI Of the Justification of Man (1571)
counter_witness.key_phrase: We are accounted righteous before God, only for the merit of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ by Faith, and not for our own works or deservings.

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Larger Catechism Q.193 on the Lord's Prayer 'give us this day our daily bread'
counter_witness.key_phrase: We pray for ourselves and others a competent portion of the good things of this life; acknowledging that we deserve them not, and pray for a holy contentment in our daily provision, not for abundance.

counter_witness.anchor: Methodist Articles of Religion, Article IX Of the Justification of Man (Wesley's 1784 abridgement of Anglican Article XI)
counter_witness.key_phrase: We are accounted righteous before God only for the merit of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ, by faith, and not for our own works or deservings.

counter_witness.anchor: Schleitheim Confession (1527) and Mennonite Confession of Dordrecht (1632), articles on suffering and the cross
counter_witness.key_phrase: The disciple is not above his master; he that will follow Christ must take up the cross and bear it, expecting tribulation and persecution in this world, not wealth and ease.

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God, position paper on financial prosperity and the gospel, and Statement of Fundamental Truths Article 5
counter_witness.key_phrase: While God in His mercy grants healing and provision according to His will, the gospel is salvation through Christ alone; the message of guaranteed material prosperity to the faithful is not biblical and is repudiated.

counter_witness.anchor: Orthodox Church in America, The Orthodox Faith, Volume IV: Spirituality (Hopko), on poverty and almsgiving
counter_witness.key_phrase: Voluntary poverty, fasting, and almsgiving belong to the heart of Christian life; the kingdom of God is not the multiplication of earthly goods but the indwelling of the Holy Trinity in the human person.

web.quote: μη θησαυριζετε υμιν θησαυρους επι της γης - 'do not lay up for yourselves treasures on the earth'
δυσκολως πλουσιος εισελευσεται εις την βασιλειαν - 'with difficulty a rich man will enter the kingdom'
μακαριοι οι πτωχοι οτι υμετερα εστιν η βασιλεια του θεου - 'blessed are the poor, for yours is the kingdom of God'
ουαι υμιν τοις πλουσιοις οτι απεχετε την παρακλησιν υμων - 'woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation'
παντα ισχυω εν τω ενδυναμουντι με - 'all things I can do in the one who strengthens me'; context vv.11-12 scopes panta to being-brought-low and abounding
ριζα γαρ παντων των κακων εστιν η φιλαργυρια - 'for a root of all kinds of evils is the love of money'
philargyria G5365: love of money, avarice; the noun appears at 1 Tim 6:10 as the rhiza of all kinds of evils
plousios G4145: 26 NT uses; heavy negative weight in Luke 6:24, Luke 12:16, Luke 16:19, Jas 1:10-11, Jas 5:1, Rev 3:17; positive only of God's mercy and Christ's becoming poor
thesauros G2344: 17 NT uses; heavenly versus earthly contrast at Matt 6:19-21, Matt 19:21, Mark 10:21, Luke 12:33-34
autarkes G842: self-sufficient, content; the lexical force at Phil 4:11 is acquired contentment in any state, not prosperity
CCC 2544: 'Jesus enjoins his disciples to prefer him to everything and everyone, and bids them to renounce all that they have for his sake and that of the Gospel.'
Sollicitudo Rei Socialis: 'The more one possesses the more one wants, while deeper aspirations remain unsatisfied and perhaps even stifled.'
Augsburg Confession IV: 'freely justified for Christ's sake, through faith… their sins are forgiven for Christ's sake.' The gospel is forgiveness.
Methodist Article IX: 'We are accounted righteous before God only for the merit of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ, by faith, and not for our own works or deservings.'
39 Articles, Article XI: the believer is accounted righteous for Christ's merit by faith, not for any works or earthly desert
Assemblies of God maintains official position papers distancing the body from the extreme prosperity-gospel teaching while affirming divine healing and provision according to God's will
OCA Orthodox Faith Volume IV: spirituality is prayer, fasting, repentance, virtues, witness in the world, and communion with God, not the multiplication of earthly goods
Cape Town Commitment 2010 names the 'toxic idolatry of consumerism' and condemns prosperity teaching within Lausanne Theology Working Group consultations (Akropong 2008-2009)
